nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; adults tend to occur in groups along ledges on steep drop-offs, generally at depths greater than 20 m; juveniles have been observed around 50 m

diet.notes: Has been documented hunting small crustaceans, worms, small bivalves, and invertebrates. Tends to accept a range of frozen meaty foods including mysis shrimp, krill, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, and chopped seafood. Quality marine flakes and pellets have also been accepted. Multiple small feedings daily are generally recommended.

summary: A small, vividly striped hogfish from the deep reefs of the Western Central Pacific, generally considered hardy and reef safe with caution around ornamental shrimp; tends to become more territorial with age.
careNotes: Pacific Redstripe Hogfish are generally considered hardy and adapt well to aquarium conditions. Juveniles tend to be peaceful, though adults have been known to bully smaller or more docile tank mates such as firefish, flasher wrasses, and seahorses. A tight-fitting lid is strongly advised — this species has been documented jumping from open aquaria. Keeping only one individual per aquarium is generally recommended unless a very large system is available for groups of five or more introduced simultaneously. The species is a protogynous hermaphrodite: individuals begin as females and the dominant specimen may transition to male. The species is usually reef safe with respect to corals and sessile invertebrates, but well-established adults have been reported to prey on smaller ornamental shrimp introduced after the fish. Ample live rock with caves and overhangs is advised both for foraging and refuge. The species has been found on deep steep-reef drop-offs (20–75 m) in the wild, so stable, well-oxygenated water with moderate to high circulation is generally recommended. Sources disagreed on adult size: FishBase lists 8.7 cm SL (estimated ~10–11 cm TL), aquarium hobby sources range from 9–15 cm; the file uses 8–12 cm as a reasonable average. Sources disagreed on temperature: retailers listed 72–78°F (22–26°C) while FishBase wild data shows 25.6–29°C; averaged to 23–27°C. Sources disagreed on minimum tank size: three retailer sources cite 75 gal (284 L), while one specialist source (ReefApp) recommends 400 L; 300 L was chosen as a cautious midpoint on the nice-values list.
breedingNotes: Bodianus sepiacaudus has been documented as oviparous with distinct pairing during breeding. It is reported to be a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite (females may transition to males). Captive breeding has not been documented in the hobby literature reviewed.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (8.7 cm SL), native range, temperature, and taxonomy. Wild temperature data (25.6–29°C) is warmer than some retailer ranges.

additional.notes: Cross-checked size (10 cm), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), temperature (23–27°C), protogynous hermaphroditism, escape risk, and compatibility notes.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (9 cm), minimum tank (400 L), salinity (1.026), diet, temperament, and hermaphroditism. Recommended larger tank than retailer sources.

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (4–5 inches), minimum tank (75 gal), temperature (72–78°F), diet, and compatibility notes.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing during breeding; protogynous hermaphrodite. No captive breeding documented in aquarium hobby sources.
